Currently, NXP trades at ~13x forward EV/EBITDA and ~19.6x forward P/E, which places it meaningfully below the diversified analog and MCU peer group, despite having an equivalent proportionate representation in automotive, industrial, and connectivity end-markets, and with a similar structural exposure to those end-markets. Texas Instruments (TXN) trades at ~21.6x EV/EBITDA and ~29.6x P/E because of its superior margin structure, pricing power, and consistent capital returns. Analog Devices (ADI) trades at ~18.3x EV/EBITDA and ~27.0x P/E, due to its higher quality of industrial margin stability, and the company's ability to expand its software content within existing systems. Microchip Technology (MCHP) trades at a significantly higher ~26.9x EV/EBITDA and ~26.3x P/E, primarily due to scarcity value and the company's highly sticky embedded customer base. At the other extreme, STMicroelectronics (STM) trades at ~9.1x EV/EBITDA and ~23.4x P/E, primarily due to higher exposures to the volatility of industrial and power cycles, while Infineon Technologies (IFNNY) trades at ~12.3x EV/EBITDA and ~21.5x P/E, due to higher exposures to the cyclical nature of the auto industry and the company's relatively heavy exposure to Europe. Therefore, considering the above-described peer group, I believe that NXP's current valuation at ~13.5x EV/EBITDA is undervalued compared to its peers, taking into consideration the company's position as a system-level player in the automotive and industrial space, improving software and connectivity content, and a structurally higher return profile than the lower-multiple European mixed signal companies.

Using estimated EBITDA of $4.96bn for FY26 and $5.66bn for FY27, and applying a 17x EV/EBITDA multiple (the peer group average), the estimated enterprise value equates to $81.8bn and $93.3bn for FY26 and FY27, respectively. After subtracting the net debt of $8.28 billion (FY26) and $8.7 billion (FY27) from these values, the estimated equity value equates to $75.1/$86.3bn for FY26/FY27, respectively.

Using 254mn diluted shares, this results in an estimated equity value of $290/$333 per share, which compares unfavorably to the current market price of ~$228. This represents ~29% to ~48% potential return (including the dividend).

Using FY26 EPS of $11.60 and FY27 EPS of $14.10, and applying a 24x forward multiple (consistent with the diversified analog peer group), the estimated equity value equates to approximately $274/$331 per share, respectively, representing ~23% to ~46% potential return (including the dividend).

Combining both the EV/EBITDA and P/E frameworks, the estimated equity value for NXP is approximately $288 and $340 per share, representing ~26% and ~49% potential return, or ~28% and ~51% potential return (including dividends) from the current levels of ~$228 per share over the next 12 to 18 months.

Risk

The primary risk for NXP is timing risk related to the recovery of the auto and industrial sectors. The company's content per vehicle is structurally strong; however, the company's production volumes are still subject to macroeconomic uncertainty and OEM inventory normalization. If the auto and industrial sector experiences a slow recovery, it may delay the time when the company achieves normalized operating leverage and margins, which will delay the earnings inflection that supports the valuation thesis. Another risk is the normalizing of prices vs. utilization. The previous upcycle experienced extremely strong pricing and mix. As supply/demand equilibrium normalizes, any renewed competitive pricing pressures, especially in industrial and communications, may limit the margin recovery unless volume absorption improves in parallel. Although China represents a significant geopolitical and demand visibility wildcard, NXP's direct exposure to categories restricted by China has decreased; nonetheless, a large portion of NXP's end demand is still indirectly dependent on Chinese manufacturing and auto production. Any new policy initiatives or substitution efforts by Chinese manufacturers could negatively impact long-cycle design wins.

Conclusion

NXP has emerged from the downcycle with a cleaner portfolio and more powerful system-level positioning than in previous downcycles. Automotive compute, industrial automation, and secure connectivity have become the structural backbone of the company and not just incremental contributors to revenue. What will determine the success of the company from this point going forward will be whether the company's earnings normalize with improved durability and a higher quality of earnings than existed in previous cycles. The company is no longer distressed, but it is still not being traded as a full structural compounder. The company should experience a meaningful return if the company is able to achieve normalized operating leverage and margins, which I believe it will.

data.title: 3 Reasons The Fed's Done Cutting Rates, For Now
data.description:
The FOMC statement that accompanies interest rate decisions is typically packed with indications on the Fed's perspective on the economy. Not the one yesterday. Even as the reserve delivered a 25 basis points rate cut yesterday, bringing them to a three year low (see chart below), the statement remained unchanged from October. And who can blame the Fed as it continues to work with limited data.

Along with the statement, though, the Fed also released its updated economic projections, which offer some significant insight. In fact, they add to arguments for an unchanged interest rates in January. So here, after a discussion on the latest rate cut, I give three reasons for why the Fed might not act on rates next month.

Unchanged perspective leads to rate cuts

The FOMC statement starts out by saying "Available indicators suggest...", much like in October. This, in a nutshell, explains why the remainder of the statement is no different either. The Fed focuses on key macroeconomic indicators like the labor market report and inflation figures, both of which are ordinarily available monthly.

However, owing to the government shutdown which ended only in mid-November, the Fed had data on both the indicators for only up to September. To be fair, the November figures are due to release later this month. But a combination of missed data for October and the timing of the FOMC meeting has once again meant that the Fed didn't have much more to work with.

In line with this, much like during the previous FOMC meeting, the Fed delivered another 25bps rate, bringing the federal funds rate to the range of 3.5-3.75%. In any case, as my article following the rate cut in September had pointed out, two more reductions were due this year.

3 reasons why January won't see a rate cut

With three consecutive rate cuts delivered by the Fed in recent months, the question now is whether it will deliver yet another cut in the upcoming meeting in January 2026. Right now, it appears unlikely. Here are three reasons why.

#1. Slowdown cancelled for 2026

Along with the latest FOMC statement, the Fed also released its updated economic projections, which have quite the element of surprise. There's limited change to the reserve's projections for GDP, unemployment rate and PCE inflation rates for 2025, the story is different for 2026.

The Fed now expects GDP to grow by 2.3% next year, a notable improvement from the earlier expectations of 1.8% and the forecast of 1.7% for 2025. For perspective, the US economy's trend growth is at some 2.2%, so technically, the expected rise next year is at above trend levels. Essentially, this means that the Fed has changed its mind on its earlier expectation that the US economy was heading towards a slowdown. It does bear mentioning though, that another rate cut is expected over the course of next year as per the projections.

Other macroeconomic indicators also look at least somewhat improved for next year. Unemployment rate is kept unchanged from previous forecast at 4.4%, but is slightly lower than the 4.5% expected in 2025. Inflation rates are expected to come in marginally lower than earlier expected, with PCE inflation forecast at 2.4% and core PCE inflation at 2.5%, which are 0.5 percentage points each lower than the expected levels for 2025.

With growth expected to be strong next year, there's less likely impetus for the Fed to reduce rates in a hurry going forward. This is even more so as the unemployment rate isn't expected to change much either. Sure, the inflation rates are expected to soften over the next year, but at still over the Fed's comfort level of 2%, an inflation decline doesn't make enough case for a rate drop.

#2. Markets show low probability of a rate cut

To start out, when I last checked on the probability of a rate cut in December, following the labor market report last month, there was just 35% chance of a rate cut. The probabilities CME Group's Fedwatch tool that assesses these probabilities based on the 30-day fed funds future rates rose to some 70% a day before the FOMC meeting. The point here is, that the likelihood can change dramatically over time.

Right now, at least, the tool shows that a rate cut is very unlikely in January. The probability of a reduction is at less than 20%. This, of course, can change, especially considering that prior to the next FOMC meeting toward the end of January 2026, two more labor market and inflation prints would have released. These could well change the expected outcomes. But so far, it appears very unlikely that another rate cut is due.

#3. Dot plot reflects lack of consensus

Even the FOMC is divided on the next course of action. The dot plot below shows the committee's participants' views on where interest rates should be next year. Unlike for 2025 or even 2027, for 2025, there's no clear consensus on where the rate should land in 2026.

The maximum number of participants agreeing on a rate is just four, compared to 12 for 2025 and a higher six for 2027. Further, the four participants each assess the three different appropriate levels. While one of this set assesses that the rate should remain unchanged, another set sees it dropping by another 25bps, while the third one actually sees an increase of 25 bps as appropriate.

Whatever the eventual outcome turns out to be, the chart does demonstrate that as of now there's unlikely to be a crying call for a rate cut. Especially after the three rate cuts already delivered, and balanced macro projections for 2026.

What's implied for the stock markets now

All key stock market indices were happy with the rate cut, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) up the most by 1%, while the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) and NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (QQQ) up by 0.7% and 0.4% respectively. This is an improvement over the mix reactions witnessed following the earlier two rate cuts. However, it still makes a limited case for short term trading based on policy rate decisions.

The next year, however, is another matter. With the Fed becoming relatively bullish on the economy in 2026, cyclicals can do better next year after underperforming this year. Sectors like real estate, basic materials and consumer discretionary have all seen at pullbacks to small increases only (see table below).

But that doesn't make them immediate buys. The representative ETFs for these three sectors, as seen in the table below, are rated anywhere between Sell and Hold. This suggests, one, that investors might want to be selective about the consumer discretionary stocks they buy. And two, keep the sector ETFs on the investing watchlist anyway for any potential rating upgrades.

In conclusion

The Fed might have delivered yet another rate cut yesterday, but the star of the show was the upgrade in GDP growth forecast for 2026. With the US economy unlikely to be in slowdown mode next year as per the latest projections, it's reasonable that FOMC participants are divided in their assessment of where interest rates should be next year. It could also explain why the probability of a rate cut next month is quite low.

Basically, don't expect a rate cut next month. At least not so far. The two labor market and inflation reports due before the next FOMC meeting will clarify more.

The latest 2026 forecast also suggests that consumer cyclicals can see a better year after limited performance, if at all, this year. The representative ETFs aren't rated Buy so far, but they are worth keeping a watch on.

data.title: Zeta Global: Bullish Catalysts Brewing
data.description:
Zeta Global (ZETA) keeps accumulating positive catalysts, which makes long-term investors like me happy to own shares of such a great company. The Marigold acquisition has been successfully completed in late November, which was the reason why the management increased FY2025 and FY2026 guidance. Therefore, we can conclude that the business combination goes on smoothly and all the expected synergies will be eventually unlocked. Recent news about platform usage was also very inspiring. Financial performance remains strong, and ZETA is still quite attractively valued even after a 4.3% share price increase since my previous call. I am adding more shares to my existing ZETA holding and confirm a Strong buy rating for the stock.

Reasons to continue buying ZETA

Zeta's financial performance remains strong, which is evident from the company's Q3 earnings release. It was a double beat versus consensus, which is always good in improving the company's image of the one that is committed to deliver on its promises. Revenue growth was solid once again with a 26% YoY increase. Operating leverage is strong because Zeta's EPS growth significantly outpaced revenue growth with a 69% growth on a YoY basis, from $0.16 to $0.27. According to Seeking Alpha, ZETA's outstanding shares count grew by just 1.5% YoY, meaning that the effect of dilution was limited on the quarterly EPS figure. Therefore, we can conclude that Zeta continues delivering high-quality EPS growth, i.e. powered by operating leverage and not some one-off events. Q3 2025 was also marked with a strong free cash flow ("FCF") growth, from $37.1 million to $57.6 million. It implies a 55% YoY growth in FCF, which underscores the high quality of Zeta's operating leverage as the EPS to FCF conversion remains quite high. To wrap up Zeta's strong Q3 earnings release, the company's financial position has improved as the net cash position increased by around $21 million sequentially.

I started my analysis with the latest earnings review because it helps the company build a rock-solid foundation for future growth. Improving net cash position together with increasing liquidity positions Zeta to reinvest in new growth projects. More investments in growth make me more optimistic as the business model has already proved itself efficient, which we see as the bottom line is growing faster than the top line. The company's R&D expenses grew by almost 67% if we compare Q3 2025 to Q3 2023, which is aggressive growth. Besides expanding organic growth potential due to increased investments in R&D, Zeta also recently proved itself as a successful business acquirer.

A few weeks ago, the company announced that it is raising financial guidance for Q4 2025 and for FY2026 after successful completion of the Marigold acquisition. Just for a brief recap, Marigold was acquired for aggregate consideration of up to $325 million to expand Zeta's global footprint and enhance AI-capabilities of Zeta Marketing Platform. I am impressed by the promptness of the positive effect on Zeta's financial performance from this acquisition. In the below table I have summarized the effect from Marigold acquisition completion on Zeta's revenue and adjusted EBITDA. The best part is that Marigold is already profitable in terms of EBITDA despite its relatively small scale. Moreover, it is also a business with solid operating leverage potential as its FY2026 adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to increase by more than two percentage points compared to Q4 2025. Clearly demonstrated early wins from Marigold's acquisition looks promising from the long-term shareholder value creation viewpoint.

Finally, recent data from the holiday shopping weekend between Thanksgiving and Cyber Monday showed that enterprises rely on Zeta's platform more aggressively. According to the release, overall usage surged by 153% YoY while Zeta's AI agent activity increased by staggering 25 times. This dynamic shows that adoption of Zeta's new offerings like agentic AI is accelerating, which underscores the company's ability to create value for customers. With successful M&A endeavors and new products developed organically, Zeta is firmly positioned to capitalize on the thriving marketing technology industry.

Last but not least, Zeta is still very attractively valued. Strong continued revenue momentum together with operating leverage is a solid combination to believe in rapid forward P/E ratio contraction. According to Wall Street analysts, the estimated 2028 multiple is just 13.7. This is a very cheap valuation for a marketing technology company with AI exposure. And it is also important to note that I am no just blindly following what Wall Street says but also have an opportunity to cross-check from the FCF side. According to the management's "Zeta 2028" strategic plan, the 2028 FCF target is $340 million. Dividing Zeta's current market cap of $4.8 billion by this forecasted FCF implies a forward estimated FY2028 P/FCF ratio of around 14. Due to the management's commitment to deliver on their targets and historically high EPS to FCF conversion, I think that the long-term forward P/FCF estimate that relies on "Zeta 2028" is a reliable one.

Reasons to avoid ZETA

Zeta accumulates vast amounts of sensitive data, which means that there is a substantial cybersecurity risk. In case of a data breach, the company's operations might be disrupted. Moreover, being a company that is unable to protect data is always bad for this company's reputation. Besides reputational risks associated with a potential data breach, there might be costly legal consequences.

The information might be particularly interesting for cybercriminals as there are dozens of large companies among Zeta's customers. Besides the cybersecurity risk, the fact that ZETA serves more than 40% of Fortune 100 companies means revenue concentration risks. I think so because companies from the Fortune 100 list are all large ones. With that being said, significant dependence on several major customers is risky because it makes Zeta's financial performance vulnerable in case one of its largest clients experiences setbacks.

In such a rapidly growing industry like digital marketing, competition risks are inevitably high. Moreover, Zeta's relatively low capital expenses every year far below $10 million per year means high probability of new market entrants since no large upfront investments are needed. Besides potential new entrants, there are digital advertising giants like Google, Meta, and Amazon. These companies are constantly looking to unlock new growth opportunities and if they decide to expand into Zeta's field it might be very difficult to compete with these giants due to their vast resources.

The verdict

ZETA is very attractively valued for a rapidly growing marketing technology business with AI exposure, while fundamentals are clearly improving. The company is well-positioned to capitalize on a favorable secular MarTech trend, meaning that ZETA remains a Strong Buy.

data.title: Cross Country Healthcare: The Failed Merger Is Disappointing, But Selling Here Would Be A Mistake
data.description:
On Wednesday, December 3rd, 2025, the stock of Cross Country Healthcare (CCRN) fell 6.16% after the company didn't release an update to their merger agreement with Aya Healthcare. December 3rd was a fallback deadline specified in the merger agreement, and CCRN was negotiating with Aya to extend it.

Then, on the morning of December 4th, CCRN announced the deal had been terminated. So we've reached the endgame: the Aya-CCRN merger has fallen through, and the stock is down 19% to $7.66 at the time of writing. It was Aya who terminated the merger, and reading between the lines, my guess is Aya did not want to pay $18.61 for a stock that was trading at roughly half that price, especially in the face of potentially more regulatory pushback.

It's a disappointing result for investors like myself who were betting on the deal completing. But it shouldn't come as a complete surprise, either. In this article, I turn our attention to CCRN's value as a standalone company as well as post-mortem the merger. My main takeaway is that two things came out of left field that may have impacted the merger: the death of Aya CEO Alan Braynin, and the recent government shutdown. I also think that CCRN is a Buy purely on fundamentals, and has multiple short-term catalysts that could cause a bounce from the 52-week lows the stock currently sits at.

Q3 2025 Earnings

Since my last article, CCRN posted their third quarter earnings report. Top line revenue came in at $250 million, down 20% YoY from $315 million and down 8.8% from last quarter's $274 million. The losses are basically exclusively in the Nurse and Allied Staffing segment, with Physician Staffing basically flat. Those are the only two revenue segments. Nurse and Allied Staffing is $668 million for the nine months of 2025, while Physician Staffing is $149 million. For 2024, those figures were $888m and $145m respectively.

The balance sheet remains strong, with $295 million in current assets compared to $130 million in total liabilities, for $165 million in tangible book value. That's roughly $5 of tangible value on a per share basis. The market cap of CCRN is $267 million at the time of writing, or $8.16 per share. The book value is $408 million, down from $419 million at the start of 2025, for a price/book ratio of 0.65. And this is without the incoming $20 million termination fee from Aya, which is about $0.60 on a per share basis.

Cash flow is also positive for the year, with $24 million in free cash flow over the first nine months of 2025. For the same time period last year, this figure was $90 million.

Using Seeking Alpha's comparison tool, I put CCRN side by side with fellow healthcare staffing stock AMN, and they both tell a similar story of dirt cheap valuations following a crash in revenues and profits. CCRN has $3 in cash per share and a 0.23 price/sales ratio. AMN has the same price/sales ratio, but unlike CCRN, AMN has a substantial amount of debt ($884 million).

CCRN next reports earnings on or around March 4th, 2026, according to Zacks.

Share Buybacks and Insider Buying

CEO John Martins gave a typical upbeat message on the 8-K announcing the termination of the merger, but what stood out was his intention to buy back CCRN shares:

Under our current repurchase program, the Company also has authority to repurchase up to $40 million of its stock from time-to-time in the open market or in privately negotiated transactions. The Company intends to immediately commence repurchases, subject to market conditions.

At the time of writing, $40 million in share repurchases would make up just under 15% of the market cap of CCRN and I think buying back shares at this depressed price is a great move by management. Insider buying has also kicked off, with director Larry Cash buying $40,000 worth of stock at $8.17 per share. I would not be surprised to see other directors follow suit.

Insiders own 5.4% of the outstanding shares of CCRN according to the latest proxy statement, with cofounder and chairman of the board Kevin Clark accounting for 2% on his own.

Takeaways From the Merger

The way I see it, there were two black swan events with the Aya-CCRN merger. The first was the passing of Aya CEO Alan Braynin in May 2025. Braynin was one half of the two CEOs who made the deal, and I outlined in my last article how it seemed that CCRN's CEO John Martins and Braynin had a close relationship. Perhaps Aya would've been less likely to back out of the deal if he were still leading the company.

But I think Braynin's death wasn't as detrimental to the merger as the second unexpected event: the longest government shutdown in US history. For a recap, I'll refer to management's update from the Q3 earnings:

As of August 29, 2025, each of [CCRN] and Aya Healthcare had certified to the FTC that it had substantially complied with the Second Request and, as a result of discussions with the FTC, the HSR waiting period was set to expire on November 17, 2025. In addition, the Aya Merger end date was extended from September 3, 2025 to December 3, 2025. However, the FTC is closed during the government shutdown and the expiry of the HSR waiting period extends day-for-day while the government shutdown continues. As a result, absent any further agreement from the FTC, the HSR waiting period is now set to expire after the end date contemplated by the Merger Agreement, which is currently December 3, 2025.

The government shutdown was 44 days long, so the expiration date for the HSR waiting period would've been pushed back to December 30. If the December 3rd deadline hadn't been passed, the FTC would've had to either push back harder on the deal, or let the waiting period expire, and the companies would've been able to merge. The government shutdown let Aya get past the December 3rd deadline and back away from the deal.

So I see these two events, the death of a CEO and a government shutdown, as things that came out of left field and hurt the deal's chances. But certainly there were more predictable signs that the deal had slim odds. A press release by the FTC confirmed what we already knew from the Second Request: that the FTC had found "significant competitive concerns" with the merger.

Conclusion

A big motto in probabilistic fields like sports or poker is "process over outcome", meaning that sometimes a good process yields bad results, or vice versa, due to luck. My "process" for CCRN was that it was a good deterministic bet on the merger going through given the price of the stock, and I roughly stand by this. We knew that mergers had lower odds of completing after getting a second request from the FTC, with about 25% of mergers finishing at that point. I thought that the merger had more than a 25% chance of going through, and that, given the intrinsic value of the underlying business as well as the potential upside, the stock had positive expected value for investors.

What I didn't anticipate was how low the stock price would go if the deal fell through. I explicitly guided towards the $9-11 range, and we ended up with lows in the $7.50 area and currently sit just above $8. I think this is overdone, and I am keeping a buy rating for CCRN.

With the $20 million Aya fee, as well as the planned share buybacks and potential for more insider buying, I see multiple short-term catalysts that could get CCRN rerated higher. In the long term, if travel nursing bounces back in any way, the business could see potential upside. Although the story with CCRN has shifted from a merger arbitrage to a deep value stock, I'll be holding my existing shares and buying more at these depressed levels.

data.title: ASML: Growth Outlook Strong, Upside Limited At This Multiple
data.description:
The Dutch chip-equipment manufacturing leader ASML (ASML) has rallied over 60% year-to-date, and now it is a more than $400 billion market-cap company. The market rewarded ASML due to consistent performance and solid top and bottom-line growth.

The semiconductor company now trades at 39x earnings multiple versus FY2025 EPS estimate, and roughly 37x versus FY2026 EPS forecast. This is not cheap. Compared to the FY2026 EPS estimate, it represents approximately a 54% premium versus peers at 24x, and nearly 61% versus the average company in the S&P500 at 23x earnings multiple. This seems to be too pricey to chase, and caps the upside in the short term.

I think that ASML is an outstanding, high-quality business, but the market already knows it. The Dutch leader has a low-leveraged capital structure, delivers steady top and bottom, while maintaining above-market margins, but valuation already reflects that.

That is why I rate ASML a Hold. I think it may appreciate in the stock price, followed by bottom-line expansion, but I also wouldn't be surprised if the stock slightly consolidates from these premium levels. I forecast a $1,176 price target over the next year, implying a 6% upside possibility, but unlikely to outperform the benchmark. It remains a solid business, but given its elevated multiple, it seems that the market already knows it.

Q3 2025: Miss On The Top-Line, But Beat On The Bottom-Line

On October 15, the company delivered Q3 2025 results. This seemed to be a mixed quarterly performance, as although ASML posted a beat on the bottom-line result, the revenue result came in below analysts' estimates. Nevertheless, the stock was up 3% on the earnings day.

ASML delivered $8.75 billion in revenue, highlighting a 7.60% growth over the past 12 months, but $224 million below what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $6.39 in diluted EPS, an 11% on a year-over-year basis versus $5.75 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.17 above analysts' estimates. I think the single-digit growth in revenue appears to be slightly concerning, and that contributes to my neutral stance. If top-line decelerates, and the trend continues in the future, the upside appears capped.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that ASML generated EUR5.6 billion in net system sales, accounting for 75% of the total revenue. The installed base management sales reached EUR2 billion. The top-line performance argues that ASML remains a well-diversified business across segments. The system sales are leading revenue performance, and it captured a split of 52% for ArF immersion revenue, while EUV lithography accounted for 38% of the total sales.

I would also like to emphasize that AMSL heavily benefited from favorable FX. The slide below highlights that on a EUR basis, the top-line performance has increased by half a percent. If no favorable FX tailwind, the company's top-line has stagnated, and it argues for my consolidation narrative.

In terms of end-use, it accounts for 35% memory and 65% logic, making it less dependent on sole end-use. China has meaningfully contributed to the top-line performance, as shipments have surged from 27% of total deliveries to 42% on a quarter-over-quarter basis. Japan, EMEA, the US, and Taiwan contracted in terms of the percentage of total contribution. Investors should monitor, as high dependency on China could lead to a discounted earnings multiple because of elevated concentration risk.

It has also indicated that ASML achieved 51.6% in gross margin, while operating margin arrived at 32.8%. The net income accounted for 28.3% of total sales. I think these profitability metrics imply an above-market performance, arguing for an elevated valuation. For instance, in terms of the net income margin (TTM) of approximately 29%, ASML operates at roughly 5.8x outperformance versus peers at 5%. If the Dutch leader can sustain premiums, I wouldn't be surprised if ASML traded at a substantial premium further.

Furthermore, the semiconductor company is also exposed to meaningful tailwinds related to broader market growth. For instance, the global lithography market was valued at $28 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to reach $55 billion by 2032. This represents a 9% CAGR, and if the company can sustain a leading position in the space, it appears to be well-positioned to benefit from the broader market expansion.

In Q3 2025, ASML has also indicated that it continues a shareholder-friendly capital approach. Besides the quarterly dividend, it has repurchased roughly 218,000 shares, resulting in about EUR148 million. Furthermore, the company anticipates the announcement of a new share buyback program in January 2026. If it includes a significant amount authorized for a share repurchase program, this may heavily support bottom-line growth over the following years.

On the upside, ASML presents a business and margin stability due to well-managed costs. Both R&D and SG&A costs remained roughly flat over the past year. However, due to top-line stagnation in EUR, investors should monitor whether costs don't pick up from here. If that happens, margins may contract, and investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the ASML stock.

To conclude, this was a mixed performance, and due to a favorable FX rate, the company's top and bottom-line growth appeared greater than it actually was. However, management guides for a strong finish to FY2025. It remains well-positioned to deliver double-digit top-line growth, but if we exclude FX, I think the result would signal high-single-digit performance. It will be interesting to see how it plays out, but at the current price tag, it seems to be difficult to justify premium expansion; thus, supporting my Hold stance.

ASML Appears Expensive, And Too Pricey To Chase

ASML currently trades at a forward P/E of about 39. Although the chart below highlights this is a business-typical valuation, given its recent performance, I argue the stock is expensive and may be due for consolidation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 2.19, ASML appears to be trading at 26% premium versus the sector median of 1.74. It remains insignificant, and may argue for uptrend continuation in the stock price, if top and bottom-line growth meaningfully resume.

On a forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 11.28 and 11.36, respectively, ASML appears to be trading at 3x overvaluation at the midpoint versus peers at 3.49 and 3.55. Valuation metrics imply that the market anticipates a meaningful top-line growth, and I don't think that high-single-digit performance, if we exclude FX, justifies a premium multiple. In addition to this, ASML remains heavily dependent on AI-driven tailwinds, and if sentiment dramatically shifts, top-line could correct, too, alongside earnings multiple.

The Dutch semiconductor manufacturing company has a low-leveraged capital structure, arguing for a bullish continuation. Although the company has $3.17 billion in total obligations, it also has $6 billion in cash. The capital structure reveals ASML could pay its debt with ease, and remains well-positioned to operate in both high and low interest rate environments. To this, following the company's reference to the expected share repurchase program in FY2026, this supports a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach, indicating strong liquidity and low leverage.

Over the past 12 months, ASML delivered a 23% revenue growth, but it's important to emphasize a few things. To begin with, the company's performance remains volatile, with one quarter posting an 11% top-line decline, which is then followed by 16% growth on a year-over-year basis. Moreover, the recent performance highlighted a slowdown in revenue growth, and investors should monitor whether this is a more structural issue. Also, long-term investors should consider FX headwind if the EURUSD pair experiences a shift in the trend.

ASML operates at premium margins, and that partially supports a premium multiple. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 53% indicates an 8% increase versus the sector median of 49%. The EBIDTA margin (TTM) of 38% highlights a 3.5x outperformance versus peers at 11%. If the semiconductor leading can sustain such margins further, this would support a bullish outlook.

The Dutch leader remains a strong company that has a solid market position and experiences momentum in demand. However, it trades at a substantial premium, which argues that the market may have already priced in the company's future growth. If results arrive weaker-than-anticipated, the downside is not protected. I remain cautious, especially due to recent performance, which would indicate a flat yearly performance, if not the FX tailwind.

Wall Street anticipates $30.15 in diluted EPS for FY2026, and this represents a 5% growth on a year-over-year basis. I agree with the estimate, as I think the company may be due for consolidation. I also think that ASML may mirror bottom-line performance; thus, if we apply approximately the current earnings multiple of 39x to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $30.15, we arrive at my price target of $1,176. It will be interesting to monitor whether the forecast plays out.

Main Risks And Concerns

ASML is tied to risks and concerns that could weigh on the stock price performance in the short term.

The company is heavily dependent on AI-powered demand. If sentiment dramatically shifts, or IT spending slows down, or the heavy Capex investments do not translate into meaningful top and bottom line growth, the AI-related names may correct. If that happens, ASML is likely to follow the trend, too.

Based on the recent quarterly performance, if we exclude a favorable FX rate, it seems that the company's top-line result was flat on a year-over-year basis. Although this could be a temporary hiccup, which is expected to pick up in the following quarter, it may also highlight a more structural issue. If the trend persists, this could significantly weigh on the company's performance and result in a correction.

The recent quarterly performance has also indicated revenue concentration risk in China. It has accounted for 42% of total shipments in the region, and if geopolitical friction emerges between the EU and China, the company's future may be clouded. In addition to this, there has been a shift in geographic trend on a quarter-over-quarter basis, with declining performance in the US, Japan, and the rest of Asia. If this isn't also a temporary hiccup, I think investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, I think ASML remains a solid player in the space, whose stock price may appreciate in accordance with its bottom-line growth.

ASML Is A Hold

Although I think ASML is a high-quality company that remains well-positioned to drive the AI-powered wave, its valuation appears to be too expensive to chase. If we exclude the FX tailwind, it may seem that it experiences a slowdown in top-line performance, and if this is a feature of a more structural issue, it may correct from premium levels.

Furthermore, based on the recent quarterly performance, it seems that the company has a revenue concentration risk and a high dependency on demand in China. If the AI-powered demand declines or sentiment around AI shifts, the company's performance may erode, and investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock. This presents a mixed picture, and I don't think it is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark in the short term.

Thus, I rate ASML a Hold with a $1,176 price target over the next year. This presents a 6% upside possibility. 38 Wall Street analysts imply a $1,062 price target over the next year, signaling a 4% downside potential. This is definitely a possibility, and it will be interesting to see how it will play out for the Dutch company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sleep Number Navigates A Challenging Consumer Market When Time Is Critical
data.description:
Sleep Number Corporation (SNBR) is wading through a challenging consumer market as new management aims to aggressively cut costs while sparking growth in the coming fiscal year. With the goal of targeting a broader range of cost-conscious consumers, the firm is revamping its product offerings while weighing a new go-to-market strategy. With its credit agreement recently amended to provide more financial flexibility in the coming quarters, SNBR is on the clock to return to profitability in order to maintain good standing. Given the weighted risks, I am recommending SNBR shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $5.64/share at 0.10x FY26 price/sales.

Though SNBR shares may be viewed as a deep value strategy, investors should be cautious as this very well may be a value trap. If electing to invest in the turnaround strategy, I recommend strict risk management procedures with a trailing stop-loss in order to protect your investment.

Sleep Number Corporation Operations

SNBR has been undergoing a major transformation in recent years as the firm has been faced with a steep decline in revenue, offloading fixed assets and leases in order to rightsize the balance sheet. SNBR announced a company-wide restructuring in Q4’23 to reduce operating expenses and improve margins. Cost-cutting measures include the termination of its strategic-development partner contract as well as lease termination and a workforce reduction. In total, SNBR has spent $81.34mm in restructuring charges since first announcing the restructuring in Q4’23. In turn, SNBR has reduced its operating costs by $115mm year-to-date, aiming to achieve $130mm by the end of FY25.

In addition to the organization, SNBR reshuffled its C-suite, bringing in a new President, CEO & Director, Linda Findley, effective April 7, 2025, and Amy O’Keefe as CFO on December 8, 2025. SNBR also appointed Phillip M. Eyler as Independent Chair effective at the conclusion of the 2025 Annual Meeting.

A core component of the restructuring is to bring to market new product offerings with the intent to reach a new customer base while simplifying its operating model. As part of this, management is expecting to offer more affordable products with features consumers are seeking out, likely offering lower-end products that provide more value.

One of the challenges SNBR may be faced with as it exits FY25 and enters FY26 is the challenging consumer discretionary environment. McKinsey & Company reported its Q4’25 consumer trends in early December 2025, suggesting that 75% of consumers are trading down in at least one category in terms of their budgets, largely driven by concerns relating to the cost of living and job security. Though SNBR primarily caters to the higher-end consumer, the firm’s refocus on the broader market might be met with some hesitancy in the near-term if consumers continue to tighten their discretionary budgets. Specific to SNBR, consumers have lowered their expectations for spending on home furnishings and furniture for Q4’25, potentially impacting SNBR’s ability to achieve its sales target for the quarter.

The employment environment may also be facing challenges going into the end of 2025. The ADP National Employment Report for November 2025 indicated a -32,000 decline in employment, largely driven by a pullback in small companies with stronger hiring across mid- and large employers. From an industry perspective, job declines were largely tied to blue collar workers in information, financial services, and professional services.

Sleep Number Corporation Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q4’25, I’m forecasting SNBR to report $325mm in net sales with a diluted EPS of -$1.48/share. Given the challenging consumer market and potential cost-cutting headwinds, I believe SNBR may face another challenging quarter with the expectation of a further decline in sales paired with improving operating margins. Given management’s aggressive cost-cutting measures, I’m forecasting SNBR to return to profitability in FY27 with FY26 being a trough year.

SNBR closed Q3’25 with $1.26mm in cash & equivalent and $579.5mm in borrowings for a leverage ratio of 6.80x net debt/aEBITDA, a steep increase from previous years driven by increased borrowings under its revolver and declining aEBITDA.

In Q3’25, management indicated that SNBR has secured an amendment to extend its bank agreement through 2027, alleviating certain risks to pay down its revolver. As part of its amended credit facility, SNBR reduced its borrowing capacity by $10mm to $475mm and will further reduce its capacity to $465mm on July 31, 2026. SNBR also gained some breathing room for its outstanding leverage covenant, increasing the cap to 5.25x through the end of FY25 and reducing to 4.75x for Q1’26, 4.80x for Q2’26, and 4x going forward. As of Q3’25, SNBR had $82mm available under its existing credit facility. As of Q3’25, SNBR had a debt-service coverage ratio [DSCR] of 1.04x, calculated as adjusted EBITDA over interest expense.

Considering SNBR’s financial position, challenging consumer market, and restructuring, SNBR may be in a challenging position in terms of bolstering operations in order to widen its debt coverage, potentially impacting the company’s ability to make interest payments on its outstanding borrowings.

As part of the restructuring program, SNBR may be rolling off more fixed assets while consolidating its real estate footprint. This may mean that the firm will have a relatively lower capital reinvestment rate with respect to depreciation in the coming quarters as the firm rightsizes its operational footprint.

A major challenge SNBR may be facing is the significant reduction in media spend, narrowing its marketing budget to cut down on costs. Though management is aiming to transition more sales to its digital platform, a reduction in marketing may create challenges at the top-line, and may hinder the firm’s ability to further reach its target audience for mattress sales. Nonetheless, the firm is aiming to lean more heavily into its sales channels more effectively, which may involve offloading marketing costs to channel partners. Theoretically, the restructuring of its revolver may provide certain flexibility to improving its marketing spend to draw in new business.

Risks Related to Sleep Number Corporation

Bull Case

SNBR is currently undergoing a major restructuring in an effort to cut costs while managing its elevated debt position. Given the significant share price pullback over the last five years, shareholders could potentially be rewarded if SNBR is successful in turning back to profitability while working down its bloated debt position.

Bear Case

SNBR faces mounting debt and interest payments that may result in increased risk of not being covered by operations. With the consumer environment weakening, SNBR may be navigating a challenging consumer market in the coming quarters that may add pressure to maintaining its debt covenants.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

SNBR shares are currently trading at 0.12x price/sales, a significant discount by historical levels. Despite the deep discount, SNBR faces certain financial risks that may further drive the share price down. SNBR’s market capitalization makes up a mere 24% of its total enterprise value, suggesting that investors may be concerned with the firm’s ability to successfully restructure and work its way out of mounting debt.

Given the challenging environment for the end of 2025, I am recommending SNBR shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $5.64/share at 0.10x price/sales.

In the instance of a successful turnaround, shares could have a potential intrinsic value of $39/share; however, SNBR shares very well could be a value trap. From a weighted risk perspective, SNBR shares may not be an appropriate investment for most investors as the turnaround may take multiple years before realizing any value.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.
